questions.question: You draw one card and are told it is a heart. Given that, what is the probability the card is red?

explanations.A: Correct. Every heart is a red suit, so conditioning on a heart restricts the sample space (13 hearts) to entirely red cards, giving probability 1.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the unconditional chance of red before any information; knowing the card is a heart removes that uncertainty.
explanations.C: 1/4 is the unconditional chance of drawing a heart from the full deck, not P(red | heart).
explanations.D: 0 would mean a heart can never be red, but hearts are by definition a red suit.

questions.question: You draw one card and are told it is a heart. Given that, what is the probability the card is black?

explanations.A: 1/2 ignores the conditioning; once the card is known to be a heart it cannot be black.
explanations.B: Correct. A heart is a red suit, so given the card is a heart the probability it is black is 0 — the two colors are mutually exclusive.
explanations.C: 1 would make black certain, but every heart is red, not black.
explanations.D: 1/4 is an unconditional suit chance, not P(black | heart).

questions.question: A bag holds red, blue, and green marbles. You are told the marble you drew is red. Given that, what is the probability it is blue?

explanations.A: Correct. The marble is already known to be red, so it cannot be blue; the probability is 0 (the colors are mutually exclusive).
explanations.B: 1/2 ignores the conditioning; you were already told the marble is red.
explanations.C: 1/3 is a guess based on three colors; the color is already known to be red.
explanations.D: 1/4 has no basis once the marble's color is given as red.

questions.question: By definition, the conditional probability P(A | B) equals which expression?

explanations.A: Correct. P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B): restrict to the outcomes where B occurs, then measure the fraction that also lie in A.
explanations.B: This omits the joint event in the numerator; conditioning measures A within B, i.e. P(A ∩ B).
explanations.C: This reverses the ratio; the conditioning event B belongs in the denominator.
explanations.D: That is the joint probability under independence, not the definition of conditional probability.

questions.question: Drawing two cards "without replacement" means that for the second draw, the deck:
options: is the same size as before the first draw
has one fewer card, so probabilities depend on the first draw

explanations.A: That describes drawing WITH replacement; without replacement the deck shrinks by one.
explanations.B: Correct. The first card is not returned, so the deck shrinks to 51 and the second-draw probabilities depend on what was removed (the draws are dependent).
explanations.C: After one card is removed there are 51 cards, not 52, and the composition has changed.
explanations.D: Without replacement the draws are dependent; the first outcome changes the second-draw probabilities.

questions.question: For two draws, the quickest way to compute P(at least one red) is usually to:
options: add P(first red) and P(second red)
multiply P(first red) by P(second red)

explanations.A: Adding single-draw probabilities double-counts the both-red overlap and can exceed 1.
explanations.B: That gives P(both red), a subset of "at least one red", not the whole event.
explanations.C: Correct. "At least one red" is the complement of "no reds at all", so P(at least one red) = 1 − P(both non-red).
explanations.D: The first draw alone ignores the chance the red appears only on the second draw.

questions.question: Conditioning on an event B that has already occurred changes a probability by:
options: keeping all outcomes but lowering every probability equally
always making the event more likely
having no effect unless the events are independent
restricting the sample space to outcomes where B is true

explanations.A: Conditioning removes the outcomes inconsistent with B rather than scaling all of them uniformly.
explanations.B: Conditioning can raise or lower a probability depending on how B overlaps the event.
explanations.C: Conditioning generally changes probabilities; it leaves them unchanged only in the special case of independence.
explanations.D: Correct. Knowing B occurred discards every outcome where B is false and renormalizes probabilities over the remaining (restricted) sample space.

questions.question: You draw a card and are told it is red. Given that, what is the probability it is a heart?

explanations.A: Correct. Conditioning on red leaves 26 cards — 13 hearts and 13 diamonds — so P(heart | red) = 13/26 = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is the unconditional heart probability in the full 52-card deck; it forgets the space shrank to 26 red cards.
explanations.C: 1/13 is a single-rank probability, not the share of hearts among the red cards.
explanations.D: 1/26 is the chance of one specific red card, not of any heart among the 26.

explanations.D: 1/26 is the chance of one specific red card, not of any diamond among the 26.

questions.question: You are told the drawn card is a face card (Jack, Queen, or King = 12 cards). What is the probability it is a King?

explanations.A: Correct. Of the 12 face cards, 4 are Kings, so P(King | face card) = 4/12 = 1/3.
explanations.B: 4/52 is the unconditional King probability in the full deck; we already conditioned on a face card.
explanations.C: 1/4 would require 4 face ranks; there are only 3 (J, Q, K), giving 1/3.
explanations.D: 1/12 is the chance of one specific face card, not all four Kings.

questions.question: Given the drawn card is a face card (12 cards), what is the probability it is a red face card?

explanations.C: 6/52 uses the full deck total; conditioning on face cards shrinks it to 12.
explanations.D: Correct. Half of the 12 face cards are red (one heart and one diamond per rank = 6 cards), so P = 6/12 = 1/2.

questions.question: You roll a fair six-sided die. Given that the roll is even (3 equally likely outcomes: 2, 4, 6), what is the probability the roll is a 6?

explanations.A: This uses the full 6-sided sample space instead of the 3 conditioned outcomes.
explanations.B: Correct. Among the 3 conditioned outcomes {2, 4, 6}, 1 fall in the event {6}, so P = 1/3.
explanations.C: This is the probability of the complementary event within the conditioned set, not the event asked.
explanations.D: This undercounts the favorable outcomes; 1 of the 3 conditioned outcomes qualify.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped twice. Given that the first flip was tails, what is the probability the second flip is heads?

explanations.A: Correct. Coin flips are independent, so the first outcome gives no information about the second: P(heads) = 1/2.
explanations.B: 1/4 is P(both flips heads); here the first flip is already fixed, so only the second matters.
explanations.C: 1/3 would apply if we conditioned on "at least one head", but conditioning on the first flip leaves the second independent at 1/2.
explanations.D: 0 would mean heads is impossible, but the second flip is an independent fair coin.

questions.question: A fair coin has just landed heads three times in a row. What is the probability the next flip is heads?

explanations.A: 1/16 = (1/2)^4 is the chance of four heads in a row from the start, not the next flip given three heads.
explanations.B: Correct. Flips are independent, so past results do not change the next flip: P(heads) = 1/2.
explanations.C: 1/8 = (1/2)^3 is the chance of the three heads already observed, not the next flip.
explanations.D: 0 reflects the gambler's fallacy; the coin has no memory, so heads is still 1/2.

questions.question: A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. You draw a marble, note its color, and put it BACK. You then draw again. Given the first draw was blue, what is the probability the second draw is red?

explanations.A: 4/9 treats the draw as without replacement; replacing the blue marble keeps the total at 10.
explanations.B: 3/9 both removes a red and shrinks the total, but nothing was removed under replacement.
explanations.C: Correct. With replacement the draws are independent and the bag is unchanged at 4 red of 10, so P(red) = 4/10 = 2/5.
explanations.D: 6/10 is the probability of blue, not red.

questions.question: A bag has 5 red and 5 blue marbles. You draw one red marble and put it BACK (with replacement). What is the probability the next draw is red?

explanations.A: This treats the draw as without replacement, shrinking the total to 9; replacement keeps it at 10.
explanations.B: This removes a red as if it were not replaced; the marble was put back.
explanations.C: This invents an extra marble; replacement leaves the composition exactly 5 of 10.
explanations.D: Correct. Replacement restores the bag to 5 red and 5 blue, so every draw has P(red) = 5/10 = 1/2.

questions.question: A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. You draw one red marble and put it BACK (with replacement). What is the probability the next draw is red?

explanations.B: This treats the draw as without replacement, shrinking the total to 9; replacement keeps it at 10.
explanations.C: This removes a red as if it were not replaced; the marble was put back.
explanations.D: This is the probability of NOT drawing red, the complement of what was asked.

explanations.A: This uses the full 6-sided sample space instead of the 3 conditioned outcomes.

explanations.B: This uses the full 4-outcome space instead of the 3 outcomes consistent with the condition.
explanations.C: This is the probability of the complement within the conditioned outcomes, not the event asked.
explanations.D: 1/2 ignores the conditioning; recount the favorable outcomes among the 3 consistent ones.

questions.question: A bag has 4 red and 4 blue marbles. You draw two without replacement. What is the probability the first is red and the second is blue (in that order)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(red then blue) = (4/8)(4/7) = 16/56 = 2/7.
explanations.B: This uses 8 for both denominators (with replacement); the second draw is out of 7.
explanations.C: This counts both orders (red-blue and blue-red); the question fixes the order red then blue.
explanations.D: 4/8 is only the first-draw red probability; multiply by the second-draw blue probability 4/7.

questions.question: Two cards are drawn from a standard 52-card deck without replacement. Given that the first card was a club, what is the probability the second card is also a club?

explanations.A: Correct. After the first card is removed, 12 qualifying cards remain among 51, so the probability is 12/51 = 4/17.
explanations.B: This uses the original 52-card deck and ignores that one card was already drawn and removed.
explanations.C: This shrinks the deck to 51 but forgets the first card was one of the qualifying cards, so only 12 remain.
explanations.D: This keeps the right count of 12 but uses 52; the deck now holds 51 cards.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. Given that at least one flip is heads, what is the probability that all three flips are heads?

explanations.A: Correct. The equally likely outcomes with at least one flip is heads are {TTH, THT, THH, HTT, HTH, HHT, HHH} (7 of them); 1 give "all three flips are heads", so P = 1/7.
explanations.B: This uses the full 8-outcome space instead of the 7 outcomes consistent with the condition.
explanations.C: This is the probability of the complement within the conditioned outcomes, not the event asked.
explanations.D: 1/2 ignores the conditioning; recount the favorable outcomes among the 7 consistent ones.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. Given that exactly two of the three flips are heads, what is the probability that the first flip is heads?

explanations.A: This uses the full 8-outcome space instead of the 3 outcomes consistent with the condition.
explanations.B: Correct. The equally likely outcomes with exactly two of the three flips are heads are {THH, HTH, HHT} (3 of them); 2 give "the first flip is heads", so P = 2/3.
explanations.C: This is the probability of the complement within the conditioned outcomes, not the event asked.
explanations.D: 1/2 ignores the conditioning; recount the favorable outcomes among the 3 consistent ones.

questions.question: A bag has 3 red and 2 blue marbles. You draw two without replacement. What is the probability of getting at least one red?

explanations.A: 1/10 is P(both blue), the complement; subtract it from 1 to get P(at least one red).
explanations.B: 3/5 is the single-draw red probability, not P(at least one red) over two draws.
explanations.C: Correct. P(both blue) = (2/5)(1/4) = 1/10, so P(at least one red) = 1 − 1/10 = 9/10.
explanations.D: This is (2/5)² (with replacement) for both blue; without replacement the second draw is 1/4.

explanations.D: Correct. P(both blue) = (3/5)(2/4) = 3/10, so P(at least one red) = 1 − 3/10 = 7/10.

questions.question: An urn holds 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Given that at least one of them is red, what is the probability that both are red?

explanations.A: Correct. P(both red) = C(4,2)/C(10,2) = 6/45. P(at least one red) = 1 − C(6,2)/C(10,2) = 30/45. So P(both red | ≥1 red) = 6/30 = 1/5.
explanations.B: 2/15 is the UNconditional P(both red); conditioning on "at least one red" shrinks the sample space.
explanations.C: This is P(at least one red), the conditioning event itself, not P(both red | at least one red).
explanations.D: This divides by C(6,2) = P(both blue) counts, the wrong restricting event.
